steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: Núñez and colleagues claim that the linear-axis tendency for time — pointing in opposite directions for past vs. future — is not universal across cultures. A supporting finding must show that Yupno speakers do not organize temporal gestures along a linear axis (i.e., past and future gestures do not point in opposite directions).

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'Yupno speakers typically use their left hand to make temporal gestures regardless of whether the gestures are past oriented or future oriented.': Hand preference (left vs. \right) is irrelevant to whether temporal gestures follow a linear directional axis. This says nothing about opposite directions for past vs. future. ✗
  • Choice B — 'Some Yupno grammatical structures used when talking about time are also used in English.': Shared grammatical structures between Yupno and English are irrelevant to whether Yupno gesture direction follows a linear-axis pattern. ✗
  • Choice C — 'Yupno speakers were observed making temporal gestures both indoors and outdoors, though with greater frequency when indoors.': Location and frequency of gesturing have nothing to do with the directional axis those gestures follow. ✗
  • Choice D — 'Future-oriented gestures used by Yupno speakers do not, on average, point in the opposite linear direction of past-oriented gestures.': This directly shows that Yupno temporal gestures lack the defining feature of the linear-axis pattern — opposite directions for past and future. That is exactly what Núñez and colleagues need to demonstrate that the tendency is not universal. ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
